questionText: Prove that $n^3 - n$ is divisible by $6$ for every integer $n$.

steps.explanation: Taking out the shared factor $n$ is the natural first step and sets up a difference of squares inside the bracket.
Since $n^2-1=(n-1)(n+1)$, the expression becomes a product of three linear factors.
Written in increasing order, these are three *consecutive* integers. That structure is the key to the whole proof.

steps.description: Split the target 6 into coprime factors.
steps.explanation: To show divisibility by $6$ it is enough to show divisibility by $2$ and by $3$ separately, because $2$ and $3$ share no common factor.
Among any two consecutive integers one must be even, so the product contains a factor of $2$.
An even factor makes the whole product even, so it is divisible by $2$.

steps.description: Set up divisibility by 3 via cases.
steps.explanation: Every integer leaves remainder $0$, $1$ or $2$ on division by $3$. Checking these three exhaustive cases settles divisibility by $3$.
If $n$ is itself a multiple of $3$, the factor $n$ supplies the divisibility.
If $n$ leaves remainder $1$, then $n-1$ is a multiple of $3$, so the factor $n-1$ supplies it.
If $n$ leaves remainder $2$, then $n+1$ is a multiple of $3$. So in every case one factor is divisible by $3$.
All three cases produce a factor divisible by $3$, so the product always is.
The product is divisible by both $2$ and $3$, and since these are coprime it is divisible by their product $6$. Hence $n^3-n$ is divisible by $6$ for all integers $n$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $n^3-n=(n-1)n(n+1)$, a product of three consecutive integers. One factor is even (so $2\mid P$) and one is a multiple of $3$ (so $3\mid P$); as $\gcd(2,3)=1$, $6\mid P$. $\blacksquare$
